skill.name: monty-code-review
skill.description: Strict Django backend code review guidance focused on correctness, tenant isolation, API contracts, performance, tests, and SOLID design tradeoffs.
skill.summary: Review Django backend changes with strict, correctness-first feedback and concrete severity tags.
skill.author: DiversioTeam

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the weak crypto, sensitive file, reconnaissance, and shell execution alerts are false positives from Markdown prose and illustrative Python examples. The skill has legitimate elevated capability because it declares Bash and Edit access, recommends tooling such as ruff and ast-grep, and requires writing a Markdown review file in the target repository.

medium_findings.title: Bash and Edit Tool Access for Repository Reviews
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash and Edit among its allowed tools and tells the agent to run or simulate tooling for changed files. This is legitimate for code review, but it gives the skill elevated local execution and modification capability in the user workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools block directly grants Bash and Edit, and later instructions mention ruff and ast-grep via Bash. I found no malicious command payload or exfiltration endpoint, so the risk is capability-based rather than intent-based.

medium_findings.title: Mandatory Markdown File Write During Review
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to write the full review into a Markdown file in the target repository. This is transparent and user-facing, but it can create or update files as part of normal operation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-writing instruction is explicit and applies whenever the skill produces a full review. The behavior is documented and limited to Markdown review output, so it is not evidence of malicious persistence.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command detections point to inline Markdown code spans, severity tags, file path examples, and Python documentation snippets. They do not show Ruby backtick execution or shell commands that execute automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown formatting or fenced Python examples, not executable Ruby or shell code. The only real execution pathway is the separately documented Bash capability.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto, Sensitive File, and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Documentation Matches
low_findings.description: The high-risk static alerts reference prose, headings, table text, or illustrative examples such as registry keys and supported platform keys. I found no cryptographic implementation, certificate material, network scanning, or system reconnaissance workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged files shows guidance text and sample Python only. Targeted search found no prompt-injection phrases, outbound endpoints, secret material, or active crypto code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Legitimate Local Tool Invocation Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends local analysis tools such as ruff and ast-grep when available. These commands are normal for code review, but marketplace users should understand that the skill may ask the agent to run local developer tooling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool invocation guidance is explicit and bounded to code review. I did not find hardcoded destructive commands, network callbacks, or user-input shell interpolation.

content.user_title: Review Django Code With Monty-Style Rigor
content.value_statement: Backend reviews often miss tenant leaks, contract drift, and unsafe migrations. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a strict Django review checklist with clear severity tags.
actual_capabilities: Reviews Django backend changes for correctness, data integrity, and tenant isolation.
Uses severity tags to separate blocking issues, should-fix items, and nits.
Checks API contracts, response shapes, exports, permissions, and privacy concerns.
Flags migration risks, N+1 query patterns, missing tests, and unsafe defaults.
Provides SOLID principle guidance using the included reference document.
Writes a structured Markdown review file when producing a full review.
limitations: It is tailored to Django backend repositories and may be too specific for other stacks.
It can be intentionally pedantic unless the user asks for a lighter review.
It depends on available repository context and cannot verify behavior without sufficient code or tests.
It may suggest local tooling, but tool availability depends on the user environment.

use_cases.title: Review a risky backend pull request
use_cases.description: Find correctness, permission, tenant scoping, and test coverage issues before merge.

use_cases.title: Audit migration safety
use_cases.description: Check schema changes for large-table locks, destructive changes, defaults, and rollout sequencing.

use_cases.title: Improve service design
use_cases.description: Use the SOLID guidance to evaluate coupling, responsibilities, interfaces, and test seams.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Django Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a normal review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use monty-code-review to review this Django change. Focus on correctness, security, tests, and clear next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Tenant Safety Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this backend diff for tenant isolation, organization scoping, permissions, exports, and privacy risks. Mark blocking issues clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Migration and Contract Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for schema or API changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use monty-code-review on this PR. Pay special attention to migration rollout safety, API compatibility, response shapes, and missing regression tests.

prompt_templates.title: Design Refactor Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this refactor with the SOLID principles reference. Identify responsibility boundaries, dependency inversion opportunities, and places where abstraction adds unnecessary complexity.

output_examples.input: A Django API change that adds a report export endpoint.
output: A short review summary describing the export behavior and main risk areas.
Blocking comments for missing organization filters or permission checks.
Should-fix comments for missing regression tests, response contract drift, or N+1 queries.
A verdict that states whether the change should merge.

output_examples.input: A migration that adds a non-nullable field to a large table.
output: A migration safety assessment that explains rollout and locking risk.
Concrete guidance to split the change into nullable add, backfill, and constraint steps.
Test and verification notes for the model behavior represented by the migration.

output_examples.input: A service refactor that introduces new platform handlers.
output: A design review focused on ownership, registries, protocols, and testability.
Feedback on whether the abstraction reduces real complexity or adds premature indirection.
Recommended tests for supported platforms and unknown-platform behavior.

best_practices: Provide the PR description, diff, and affected files so the review can judge intent and contracts.
Ask for a quick pass when you only want blocking and should-fix issues.
Review generated Markdown before committing it, especially when local tooling was run.
anti_patterns: Using the skill for frontend, mobile, or non-Django systems without adapting the guidance.
Treating every nit as a merge blocker when the user requested a lighter review.
Running suggested local commands without understanding their effect in the current repository.

faq.question: What kind of code is this skill best for?
faq.answer: It is best for Python and Django backend code, especially multi-tenant applications, APIs, exports, and migrations.

faq.question: Does it always write a file?
faq.answer: For a full review, the skill instructs the agent to write the review into a Markdown file in the repository.

faq.question: Can I ask for a shorter review?
faq.answer: Yes. Ask for a quick or non-pedantic pass to focus on blocking and should-fix issues.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: It may suggest or run local review tooling when the host agent has Bash access and the user context allows it.

faq.question: What are the severity tags?
faq.answer: It uses blocking, should-fix, and nit tags to separate merge blockers from important improvements and small cleanup items.

faq.question: Does it replace human review?
faq.answer: No. It helps structure review, but maintainers should verify findings against product requirements and production constraints.
